title: Hummus

ingredients.title: Ingredients (for 6 servings)
items: Fresh lemon juice – 80 ml (⅓ cup), strained
Garlic – 1 large clove, crushed with a pinch of salt
Ice water – 80–100 ml (⅓–½ cup), very cold
Extra virgin olive oil – for serving

ingredients.title: Garnish (classic Arabic style)
items: Optional: whole cooked chickpeas, toasted pine nuts, sumac

instruction.title: Soak the chickpeas
steps: Rinse and soak chickpeas in plenty of cold water with ½ tsp baking soda overnight (at least 12 hours).

instruction.title: Cook the chickpeas
steps: Drain and rinse. Place in a pot with fresh water and ½ tsp baking soda.
Bring to a boil, skimming off foam.
Reduce to a simmer and cook until very soft (about 1 – 1.5 hours).
Chickpeas should be almost falling apart – this creaminess is key.

instruction.title: Prepare garlic-lemon base
steps: In a small bowl, mix lemon juice with crushed garlic and a pinch of salt.
Let sit 10 minutes, then strain to remove solids (for a refined flavor).

instruction.title: Make the tahini sauce
steps: In a food processor, blend tahini with strained lemon juice.
Slowly drizzle in ice water while blending until light, pale, and creamy.

instruction.title: Blend the hummus
steps: Add warm chickpeas and salt to the tahini mixture.
Process until completely smooth, adding a little more ice water as needed.
The texture should be airy, silky, and spreadable – not heavy.

instruction.title: Taste and adjust
steps: The flavor should be nutty, tangy, and deeply savory.

instruction.title: Serve
steps: Spread hummus in a shallow bowl with the back of a spoon, creating a swirl.
Drizzle generously with top-quality olive oil.
Garnish with paprika, parsley, and optional toppings (pine nuts, whole chickpeas, or sumac).

chefTips: Always use dried chickpeas (canned won’t achieve the same texture).
Ice water is the secret to extra creamy, whipped hummus.
Quality tahini makes or breaks the recipe – choose a Lebanese, Palestinian, or Israeli brand.
Serve at room temperature, never cold from the fridge.
